glossary.ᐃᓄᒃᑎᑐᑦ (Inuktitut): The Inuit language of Nunavut. Written in a syllabic script. Syllabics were adapted by James Evans for Cree in 1840 and adapted for Inuktitut by Anglican missionaries in the 1860s. Today, many Nunavut Inuit communities maintain syllabics as their preferred writing system.
glossary.Pilimmaksarniq: IQ-05: The Inuit principle of developing skills through practice, observation, and patient mentorship. The foundation of the Heritage Skills approach to learning.
glossary.OCAP®: Ownership, Control, Access, Possession — the First Nations data sovereignty principles established by the First Nations Information Governance Centre (FNIGC). OCAP® is a registered trademark of FNIGC.
glossary.Anishinaabemowin: The Ojibwe language of the Anishinaabe peoples of the Great Lakes region (also called Ojibwe or Ojibway). A polysynthetic Algonquian language with significant regional dialect variation. Anishinaabemowin is the subject of active revitalization efforts in many Anishinaabe communities.
glossary.Tunnganarniq: IQ-02: The Inuit principle of fostering good spirit by being open, welcoming, and inclusive. Guides the relationship-building phase of oral history interviews.
glossary.corn pone: Appalachian cornbread made without eggs or milk, traditionally baked in a cast-iron skillet or Dutch oven. A staple food of Appalachian communities since colonial settlement.
glossary.qulliq: An Inuit soapstone or pottery lamp burning seal oil, used for heating, cooking, and light. The qulliq is traditionally maintained by Inuit women and holds deep cultural significance in Inuit domestic and spiritual life. The qulliq lamp is covered in Room 14 (Arctic Living).
glossary.wampum: Shell beads crafted by Haudenosaunee and Algonquian peoples, woven into belts that serve as records of treaties, agreements, and oral history. Wampum belts are living documents maintained by specific nations; they are not decorative items. Covered in Room 12 (History/Memory).

resourceDirectory: First Nations Information Governance Centre (FNIGC) — fnigc.ca — authoritative source for OCAP® framework
Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami (ITK) — itk.ca — National Inuit organization, NISR framework
Oral History Association (OHA) — oralhistory.org — professional standards for oral history practice
Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage — festival.si.edu — documentary standards
Foxfire Fund — foxfire.org — Appalachian oral history and heritage skills resources
Inhabit Media — inhabitmedia.com — Inuit-owned publisher of Inuit knowledge and literature
The Mishomis Book — recommended First Nations resource for Anishinaabe cultural context
fairUseNotice: The sources cited in this Heritage Book's bibliography are used under fair use principles for educational, non-commercial documentation of cultural heritage. All citations include creator-first purchase links directing readers to support authors and community publishers directly. For Indigenous-sourced works, fair use is further constrained by OCAP® principles: the knowledge owner retains control over how their knowledge is used, and educational use does not override community consent requirements. If you wish to reproduce content from this book beyond personal study, contact the knowledge holders or their community organizations directly.
